skill.name: graphite-cli
skill.description: Guides developers through Graphite CLI workflows for stacked pull requests, including setup, branch creation, submission, synchronization, conflict handling, and stack restructuring.
skill.summary: Use Graphite CLI commands to manage stacked pull requests from setup through merge cleanup.
skill.author: Calel33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby backtick execution and weak cryptography alerts. Manual review found these are Markdown command examples and ordinary Graphite or Git terminology, not executable Ruby or cryptographic code. The skill remains medium risk because it guides commands that can authenticate to GitHub, rebase branches, amend commits, and publish pull requests.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Mutating Graphite Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk: the skill teaches gt auth, gt create, gt submit, gt sync, gt modify, gt merge, and stack rewrite commands. These commands are legitimate for a Graphite CLI guide. They can authenticate to GitHub, amend commits, rebase branches, push PR updates, and alter local branch state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly document Graphite and Git commands that change repository and remote PR state. The context is legitimate documentation, so the risk is operational misuse rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: GitHub Authentication and Remote PR Submission
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for network-related workflow risk: the guide instructs users to authenticate Graphite with GitHub and submit or open pull requests. This is expected behavior for the tool, but agents should not run these commands without explicit user approval and repository context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands intentionally interact with GitHub and remote branches. No evidence shows credential exfiltration or unauthorized network calls beyond normal Graphite CLI behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Ruby Backtick Alerts Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the reported backtick patterns are Markdown inline code and fenced command examples, not Ruby shell execution. No Ruby files, dynamic shell evaluation, or executable wrapper code were found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is prose and Markdown command formatting. There is no executable Ruby code path that could interpret these backticks.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the high-severity weak cryptography alerts map to ordinary Graphite CLI prose and table lines. No weak algorithm use, cryptographic API, hashing routine, or security-sensitive cipher configuration was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation text and command tables only. The cited lines do not contain cryptographic algorithms or code that could weaken encryption.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URL
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious network behavior: the URL points to Graphite documentation for additional context. It is not part of executable code and does not perform a network request by itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only hardcoded URL is a plain documentation link in Markdown. No fetch, curl, webhook, or data transmission logic is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Operations Documented in Markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains numerous gt and git command examples. They are not embedded executable scripts, but users should review working tree state and remote targets before allowing an agent to run them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly listed and can affect repository state. They are safe as documentation but need approval before execution in an agent workflow.

content.user_title: Manage Stacked PRs with Graphite CLI
content.value_statement: Large pull requests slow review and make dependency changes hard to track. This skill guides Graphite CLI workflows for creating, submitting, syncing, and restructuring stacked pull requests.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to initialize Graphite in a Git repository and select a trunk branch.
Guides creation of stacked branches with gt create and related aliases.
Describes how to submit single PRs or full stacks for review.
Covers stack navigation commands such as gt up, gt down, gt top, and gt bottom.
Explains synchronization, restacking, conflict continuation, and cleanup workflows.
Summarizes advanced stack operations including move, fold, split, absorb, and multi-trunk setup.
limitations: It provides procedural guidance and does not include executable automation scripts.
It assumes Graphite CLI is already installed or can be installed from official documentation.
It cannot verify repository status, branch dependencies, or GitHub permissions without tool access.
Some command details may change as Graphite CLI evolves.

use_cases.title: Break a Feature into Reviewable PRs
use_cases.description: Plan a stack of small dependent changes, create each branch with Graphite, and submit the stack for review.

use_cases.title: Recover a Stack After Trunk Changes
use_cases.description: Use sync, conflict resolution, and continuation steps to restack branches after new changes land on trunk.

use_cases.title: Restructure Existing Work
use_cases.description: Choose move, fold, split, insert, or absorb workflows to reshape a stack before final review.

prompt_templates.title: Start with Graphite
prompt_templates.prompt: I am new to Graphite CLI. Walk me through initializing a repository, choosing trunk, and creating my first branch.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Stack Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me split this feature into three stacked pull requests and map each step to Graphite CLI commands.

prompt_templates.title: Update After Review
prompt_templates.prompt: I received feedback on the middle branch of my stack. Explain how to modify it and update the rest of the stack safely.

prompt_templates.title: Repair a Complex Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: My Graphite stack has conflicts after sync and one branch should move under a different parent. Diagnose the safest command sequence.

output_examples.input: How do I submit all branches in my current stack?
output_examples.output: The response explains moving to the top branch, reviewing the stack log, submitting the stack, and checking each created pull request.

output_examples.input: What should I do when sync stops on a conflict?
output_examples.output: The response lists conflict resolution steps, staging resolved files, continuing the Graphite operation, and verifying the stack afterward.

output_examples.input: When should I use fold instead of split?
output_examples.output: The response compares combining a small branch into its parent with separating a large branch into smaller review units.

best_practices: Check the current branch and stack log before running commands that rebase or submit work.
Submit draft PRs early when a stack needs feedback before it is ready to merge.
Run sync before final review or merge so branches are based on current trunk.
anti_patterns: Do not run submit, merge, move, fold, or split without confirming the intended branch and remote target.
Do not use one large branch when the work can be divided into clear dependent PRs.
Do not ignore conflicts during sync because unresolved restacks can leave the stack inconsistent.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Graphite commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command explanations. Any command execution depends on the host AI tool and user approval.

faq.question: What is the main Graphite CLI command prefix?
faq.answer: Graphite CLI commands use the gt prefix, such as gt create, gt submit, gt sync, and gt log short.

faq.question: Can it help with GitHub pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how Graphite creates, updates, opens, and submits pull requests for stacked branches.

faq.question: Does it cover conflict resolution?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes resolving conflicts, staging the resolved files, and continuing the paused Graphite operation.

faq.question: Can it explain advanced stack changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers moving, folding, splitting, inserting, absorbing, freezing, tracking, and multi-trunk workflows.

faq.question: What should users verify before running commands?
faq.answer: Users should verify the current branch, working tree state, intended trunk, target remote, and whether commands will publish or rewrite branches.
